speaker: interviewer
text: Today is January 21, 1997. I'm here to interview Marianne. Or her name that she goes by is Manci Rosner. Her maiden name is Robitschek. My name is Nancy Solomon. This is Hallandale Beach, Florida, United States. And the language we're speaking is English.

speaker: interviewee
text: Marianne, or Manci, Rosner--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --thank you so much for doing this interview.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're very welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Nancy Solomon, as we said, and this is Marianne, Manci, Rosner. And I thank you so much. And we appreciate the testimony you're going to give.

speaker: interviewee
text: You are very welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for joining us, Marianne. We appreciate you coming to give us your testimony. Let's start off by finding out the name that you were given at birth. Your first name is Marianne?

speaker: interviewee
text: Marianne.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rosner, born Robitschek.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: R-O-B-I-T-S-C-H-E-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: Manci, what was-- what town were you born in, what city?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Vienna-- Vienna, Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the year?

speaker: interviewer
text: And give me your full birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: 21st of October, 1910.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your home like when you were born? Did you have a lot of brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had only one sister--

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: --two years older. Her name was Gertrude.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my mother's name is Ernestine Robitschek, born Guth.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's spelled?

speaker: interviewee
text: G-U-T-H.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Born in Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father is Max Robitschek, born in Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live within an extended family there? Were there a lot of grandparents or relatives around you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember a lot of aunts and uncles and cousins. And I remember only grandmothers. Grandfathers, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were your grandmother's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: One was Fannie Guth. And the other one, believe it or not, I don't remember her first name, Robitschek.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was-- in-- in-- in Vienna they called this a [NON-ENGLISH]. He owned a cafe house, a very nice, large, elegant cafe house in Wien, Vienna, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of home did you live in? Was it a modest home? Was it a comfortable home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very, very comfortable. My father had a very prosperous business, and I would say we were in-- very well to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have-- your neighborhood, what kind of neighborhood was it? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mixed neighborhood. It was, I mean, a beautiful neighborhood and where I had lived in a beautiful apartment building. We an eight-room apartment and very, very comfortable, servants, governesses, private teachers, piano lessons, language lessons, dancing schools, the most beautiful childhood a kid can ever had, I had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother work as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- she helped my father in his business, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: About Vienna, just briefly in that time that you were living there as-- as a little girl growing up.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's just beautiful, just beautiful, a beautiful city. And, believe it or not, when I was young, when I was home, I didn't feel the anti-Semitism [? was what ?] the people were talking now about it. I didn't feel it. I have felt-- we were-- I had mixed friends, and-- and every one not religious Jews. We were Jews, but not religious. And I had-- my best friends was Christians, Catholics. It didn't make any difference.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you didn't feel any acts of anti-Semitism as you were growing up? Didn't see any?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't feel anything. I left Vienna in 1930 when I got married. Until then, I didn't feel it, not at all. It must have come later. Then I think the-- Hitler-- Hitler came to Vienna, I think, in 1936, '37, something like this. So then I was no-- I was already not there.

speaker: interviewer
text: The neighborhood that you were living in, was it a mixed neighborhood of Gentiles and Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mixed, mixed, mixed, mixed.

speaker: interviewer
text: As you were getting older, growing up, did you hear any kind of talk about what was happening in Germany? Did you hear any kind of talk about things that were not right politically?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I did not. I did not. I don't know-- did my parents cover this somehow? I did not. I did not. The first-- my first meeting with anti-Semitism was in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll talk about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as--as you were growing up, tell me about your schooling. How far did you go in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I had two-- two classes of gymnasium. I didn't want to learn. I was a pretty girl, and I like boys. I liked them, and I didn't want to study. I didn't want to-- I was a bad girl. I was not a good kid when it came to school. I didn't want to learn at all. So I learned a profession. My mother says, you don't want to study, so you learn a profession. And she took me to her dressmaker, and she paid her, and she teach me how to-- how to become a dressmaker. Every day I was in her salon. And every day, I went to school. And I became a dressmaker. I became a master dress-- I mean, the-- I mean, the license meant everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you started learning at what age would you say, the dressmaking?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I started to [? make ?] at 15 or 16. And I-- had-- this course was for two years, and then I-- I-- I opened my own business in our apartment. My mother gave me two rooms. And I had girls working for me and-- till I got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is 19-- let's say 1926 to '28?

speaker: interviewee
text: Something like this. Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So about-- was it around 18? Or what age was it that you were on your own, and you started your own business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, about at 17, 18 years old, yes. In-- in Vienna, you could-- my father made me mature. He-- we went to a-- to a-- to a city hall, you know. He said, he declared that I am full of age. And I got the license as a dressmaker as a master. And I had a little sign on the door with my name on it. I was very successful. Of course, I didn't have no overhead. I paid no rent. I paid no electricity. I paid for nothing, so I was successful. It was very easy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of people came to you to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly friends of my mother. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of things did you make?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dresses, blouses, skirts, no slacks. This time, it wasn't-- it wasn't in style for ladies to wear slacks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you do this for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until I got married. My-- my wedding gown was-- was sewn in my shop. My girls that worked for me, they sew this gown for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Matter of fact, they sewed with some hairs they pulled out, and they sewed. They said, when you-- when you sew in your hairs, then you get married. We wanted to get married, so that's what we did. It was a superstition, you know? Now how did you meet your husband? And what was-- what was your husband's name-- your fiancee at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband's name was Henry, Henry Rosner. He was playing in a very elegant restaurant not far from our coffee house. Well, my father, once in a while, took us there, my sister and myself, for dinner. And was starting to flirt, once he found out who I am, and that-- that's how we got acquainted. That's how it started. I was 14 years old when I met my husband. And then he left me, and he studied in Vienna. He was in the [? conservatorium. ?] And then he left Vienna, and we are-- he was writing to me. I was writing to him, and then he came visit Vienna. And-- and we fell in love. We were very much in love, and in 19-- 1929, I got an invitation from his parents to come to Poland, to Krakow. My mother went with me when I got engaged.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you surprised at the-- did you know it was going to happen when the invitation came?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the invitation came, I had the suspicion, yes. Yes, and all the rest. Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the day that that happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know it was in the year 1929.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what you felt like when that was--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very excited, yes. Yes. I gave-- I-- I got the beautiful ring from my father-in-law, he gave to me. At this time he was not my father-in-law yet. From my husband's father, I got the very nice engagement ring. And I had nothing for my husband there, but then later on my parents gave him a golden watch, I remember. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were married in what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1930, 12th of August, 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you married? What city were you married in?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the wedding? Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very, very simple, very-- very small wedding because my mother didn't-- was not for the wedding. My mother was very chauvinistic. She liked only what is Austrian and Viennese. Well, my-- my husband was Polish, and he was a musician. And to him, that was like a Gypsy. She didn't-- she was not-- so we had only in a small temple. We had the ceremony, and then we had a little family dinner. That's all. And then we left. We left right away for-- for Czechoslovakia, for Reichenberg. My husband had a contract there as a musician.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did he play? What instrument?

speaker: interviewee
text: Violin. He was a very fine violinist.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when he went to Czechoslovakia, how long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three months, all contracts were always from two   or three months. We traveled. We traveled until 19-- 19-- 1933, '34, something like this. We traveled Poland, Romania, or Czechoslovakia, Hungaria. We traveled. He had-- he had an agent who made the contracts for him. We traveled, which made me very unhappy. It was a very tough life for me because you make friends, and after you are friends, you had to leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the-- this time that you were traveling between 1930, 1933, '34, what signs were you seeing of what was going on in Germany with Hitler?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see too much. I didn't see too much because I didn't-- I was so young. You know, I was not-- not quite 19 years old when I got married, and I didn't see too much. It-- I started to see in-- when I went-- we went to Poland. Then-- then I saw that there were already refugees there from Germany, which-- which they left Germany because of Hitler.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year would you say that that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in 1934, 1935. 1935, my son was born in Warsaw. Then we settled down. I had an apartment in Warsaw, and before that already in Katowice, I-- I felt. I felt-- I saw and I felt the anti-Semitism and I felt the anti-Jewish situation very badly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there people around you? Were there signs that told you to do certain things? Were there notices given out to Jews at that point too?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Yes, there were signs on the street, big signs over the street. You shouldn't buy anything by Jews. There was signs on stores, Jews and dogs is the entrance forbidden. You know, in-- in-- in one town, in Bydgoszcz, when my husband had the contract, he had to change his name. They called him [? Hermanio ?] [? Rosenetti ?] because his born name was Herman. Herman Rosner was too Jewish for them. So maybe I felt it. There [? is-- ?] I felt it.

speaker: interviewer
text: During this time, Manci, was there ever any discussion with you and your husband about leaving Poland because of the anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we didn't want to live in Poland. We wanted to go back to Austria. My father thought because there was no boys, that my husband is in this business, understands it. So after a few years, we'll go around, travel around, then we're gonna come back to Austria. But it came to a time we couldn't go back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why not?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we had to go to the ghetto in-- in Poland. And then we came to a concentration camp. In 1939, the war broke out. You couldn't go back anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me about the time, like, between after your son was born in 1935. And was your father asking you to come to-- back to Vienna then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he didn't. We had a very good life. My husband made very good money as a violinist. I had a very good life. And, no, we didn't-- we-- we were not ready to go back yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: So even though you felt anti-Semitism, at that point, you weren't ready to--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no

speaker: interviewer
text: --to make the move?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I mean, the anti-Semitism was all around, but it wasn't directly personal to me. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- tell me about what happened between 1935 and 1939, and-- where you were living, in terms of it getting increasingly worse.

speaker: interviewee
text: In this time, we heard that the, for example, children couldn't go to university anymore, Jewish children. Jewish boys or girls couldn't study. When they wanted to study, they had to go out of the country. And Jewish positions, high positions, were not acceptable. Teachers and-- and in the government couldn't work anymore. And then in 1939, the war broke out. And then it was finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of neighborhood where you living in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was living in a very nice mixed neighborhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have neighbors that-- who were Gentile who spoke to about what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nobody spoke to us about it. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear any radio reports about what was happening in Germany or read any newspapers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. Sure, we had learned that the German Jews had to leave Germany. We-- we heard about that, but we-- you know, we didn't think that it could touch us. We didn't believe in that, that it could happen to us. Especially me, I was Viennese. Vienna was my city. I loved it. I loved my city.

speaker: interviewer
text: As it got closer to 1939, did your father say to you, it's now time to come out?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, he did not. He did not say that, in the opposite. My mother died. My mother died in 1934 on diabetes. And I came to Vienna to-- to the funeral. And then I came here to Vienna with my son. He was three years old in 1937. I visited my father. And I-- my father was, at this time, already a widower. And I-- I asked my father to come with me to Poland and live with us in Poland. I thought he's gonna be safer there, but he didn't want to. He didn't want to. He said, an old tree, you shouldn't replant. He didn't want to go. But he had to leave. He had to leave. And I don't know when. I don't exactly know when he left. He left to Czechoslovakia when he was with his brother. And then he came to Theresienstadt, which was a concentration camp in-- in Czechoslovakia. And from there, they shipped him to Auschwitz. And there he got finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me what's happening now, Manci, as 1939 is approaching and-- and the war is--

speaker: interviewee
text: 1939, The war caught us. My husband was on a contact in Krakow, and all of us, we didn't expect that. When-- when the first bomb was falling, we didn't believe it. We thought that [INAUDIBLE] something. We didn't believe it. And then there was the-- the-- the signal for us to go to shelter. We went to the shelter. And the bombs came down. Krakow was pretty much bombed, and a lot of people left. You know, they fled.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I had a little boy. My boy was-- oh, it was, he was born in-- four years old, so how far can I go with him? Money, we didn't have too much either because we used to live very nicely. My husband made very nice money, but we spended it. So I think, where can I go? So we stayed on. And we had to go from one ghetto to the other. We went back to Warsaw, and there we couldn't live anymore in our apartment. We had to get to a section where Jews are concentrated. Before we get there, just tell me a little bit more when-- when the war broke out in Krakow. Tell me about that first day and what you remember seeing and hearing.

speaker: interviewee
text: The first day, we went to the shelter. We were sitting there until bomb-- the bomb signal of the bombs went off. And then we went upstairs to our-- I had only a furnished-- furnished rooms. And my-- I had a girl, like a-- for-- for the-- for my boy, so she came from Katowice. I said, go home. Maybe home, you're going to be more safe. She was not Jewish, so I said, you are not safe with us. You better go home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she left, and we were there a short time. And then we are-- it was very, very hard, very hard. It was-- we couldn't go nowheres, and we had to wear armbands with the David star on it. And you couldn't go to stores, so it was a very, very tough life, very tough. Did all-- what happened to your husband and his-- and his being a musician, a violinist? Did that stop?

speaker: interviewee
text: He couldn't-- he stopped. He couldn't play anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you live, day to day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Day to day. We lived day to day. We ate up our-- we ate from our little savings, which we [? ate ?] until we left Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the authorities coming in and telling you to do certain things at that point? Were there signs posted? Where there--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they [INAUDIBLE] Krakow, I remember that in the middle of the night there was knocking on the door, very hard knocking. And they came, and they took us. And they put us in a-- in some kind of a school or somewhere, and then they put us on a train. And the-- the train went somewhere. In the middle of the night, the train stopped. It was winter. It was maybe November, November 1939. And the train stopped in-- in-- on a field. The snow was up to the knees, and we didn't know where we are. And the train left, and they left us. We were not only alone. There were maybe a hundred people. When we were standing there in the snow, we didn't know, so we decided to walk. We walked, and we walked. And we saw some lights. We knew that some people must live there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we knocked on the door, a few houses, and they-- they took us in. They were nice people, good people. And we spent the night there. And the next day, we-- we tried to go to some train station, and we went back. So you never knew why--

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and we never knew where we were. We found out-- we found out wherever. I don't remember the name of it, a small town somewhere about four or five hours away from Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they rounded people up in the middle the night to do this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they took us together, about a hundred people, they took all of their homes in the middle of the night. And they transported us. And I think they did something which they're not supposed to do. I think they let us go. They're not supposed to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did most people go back to Krakow? Or did some flee into some other city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, some flee. Some went back. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your decision to go back was based on--

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know what to do. We didn't-- I had a little boy with me, four years old. What would-- where should I go? So we tried to go back, or we went back.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when you went back? How much longer were you staying there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too long, not too long. Then we went back to Warsaw. And then we-- we came in the ghetto in Warsaw. And then-- then we--

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me. Tell me, but how did you get from Krakow to the ghetto. And what was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were you notified about that? How did they tell you to-- to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went on our own. We went on our own. We didn't-- we had to go there. There were signs and things that said, all Jews have to move there-- there-- there. And they gave us a room. They gave us-- I couldn't go to my apartment. My apartment was not in the ghetto. They gave us a room somewhere down [INAUDIBLE] Street in [INAUDIBLE]. I remember like it was yesterday.

speaker: interviewer
text: When we moved in, we were there. This was the ghetto. My husband played there in the ghetto in a-- in a restaurant for the Jews, you know? When you say you couldn't go to your apartment, was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know you couldn't go? Was it boarded up? Or was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. It was forbidden. Forbidden. There were signs that you-- you can go only in this and this district.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you were able to take things out of your apartment with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, nothing. Everything was there. Everything was lost.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1940 maybe, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the month or the season?

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been the winter, probably in the wintertime, I image, yeah. I imagine in the wintertime. Then we wanted to-- then they came. They almost surrounded the-- they built a-- a wall around the ghetto. And they were going to close up the ghetto. So we didn't want to be in the closed ghetto, so we ran away. We-- we took off our things, and we-- we went to the train, and we  went back to Krakow. And my-- in Krakow, we couldn't be anymore. My in-laws were in a small town called [INAUDIBLE], so we went there.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just want to make sure that I have this straight in terms of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the chronology of things. So when you went-- on the first trip that you took on that train and in the middle of the night they left you in the field, you went back to your place in Krakow. Is that right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: From Krakow, did you go to the Krakow ghetto at that point? Or did you go to the Warsaw ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, the Krakow ghetto came much later.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's what I thought. So after--

speaker: interviewee
text: After this, there was a-- there was a rule that the Jews had to live 30 kilometers away from Krakow. So my-- my in-laws were already in a little town, so we went there. We went there, and we were living there for a few months by some Polish farmers in a room till-- till the ghetto time. Then game the rules that all Jews had to move to Krakow to the ghetto. So we had to move to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that-- And that was 1940 in the winter?

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- that was maybe 1940, 1941 probably.

speaker: interviewer
text: Those relatives in-- that kept you in this-- in the town till you had to move to the ghetto, what did you do during-- during that time with them? Were you just hiding or staying with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just hiding, just being quiet and not be seen too much. And I had some-- I had-- a little bit of money, of course. We had to pay for the-- the farmer for our staying there. And then somebody, some of the Polacks, they found out that my husband is a violinist and my brother-in-law is an accordionist. So they-- they went to some when the-- when there was the-- when they take out the-- I don't know how you call it when-- when you take the fruit from the-- at the end of the season. There were always games and entertainment.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took my husband and my brother-in-law to play for them. And they gave them potatoes and flour. They gave them something, so somehow we-- somehow we survived. When I think back to this over 50 years, I can't believe-- believer-- I don't believe it myself. We'll stop here and continue on the next tape.
